system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

Two strategies emerged: **Strategy A — Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json`): For the HOME PAGE, pre-resolve all data at build time. The resolver handles `$img` refs, locale objects, and different data paths. Components import the resolved JSON (4KB) instead of the raw content (80KB). **Strategy B — Inline data for sub-pages** (programs, faq, blog, about): These pages use hardcoded arrays because the 80KB content structure has items in paths like `programasPage.programs` (dict, not array of items), FAQ items in `faqPage.full` (non-standard format), and blog posts in `blog.index.posts` (empty array in the JSON — actual posts are MDX files in a `blog/` directory). **When to use which:** ```typescript // Strategy A — content matches component expectations // Use resolved.json when you can flatten the data paths // Strategy B — content structure doesn't match // Use inline arrays when: // - Content JSON has empty arrays for expected fields // - Data is stored in objects instead of arrays // - Items use non-standard field names // - Content exists in external files (MDX, separate JSON) ``` **Blog post page with Next.js 15 async params (no MDX):** ```tsx // app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx const posts = [...]; // inline array export default async function BlogPost(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await props.params // MUST await params in Next.js 15 const post = posts.find(p => p.slug === slug) if (!post) notFound() // ... render post } ``` **Blog cards linking to [slug]:** ```tsx // app/blog/page.tsx — cards must be not

When subagent calls fail, fall back to sequential execution directly in the main session. The subagent system uses a different model (claude-sonnet-4 by default) which may be misconfigured or hitting rate limits. If `delegate_task` returns `Error code: 401 - {'error': {'message': 'User not found.'}}`, debug with `hermes config get models` or work around with terminal + `python3 << 'PYEOF' ... PYEOF` for batch operations. 1a. **Navigation structure varies by tenant — NEVER assume array shape.** Different tenants store nav in completely different structures. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/s/en/nexa-paraguay" }] } ``` **Fix:** Check with `typeof content.navigation`. For object with `navItems`, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. For array, use `c.navigation || []`. 1b. **Nav hrefs may contain PAB paths (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas`).** After fixing the structure (step 1a), the href values themselves are wrong — they reference old PAB routes. Batch-replace in the header component: ```typescript const href = (n.href || n.path || "").replace("/s/en/nexa-paraguay", "") || "/" ``` Without this, clicking a nav link goes to `/sobre` (correct) but `prefetchNextRouter` on the `` prefetches the munged path (e.g. `/object Object`) causing `[object Object] 404` errors in console. The same `.replace()` must be applied in BOTH desktop and mobile nav loops. 1c. **PAB nav href prefixed with `/` + `.replace()` creates `//s/en/...` paths.** If your code does `"/" + href` BEFORE the replace, the link becomes `"//s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas"` which Next.js renders as `[object Object]`. The order must be: extract href → strip PAB prefix → prepend `/` if empty. 1d. **`@/` import alias may fail in freshly scaffolded apps.** The `@/*` path alias in tsconfig.json may not resolve correctly if `tsconfig.json` has `baseUrl` misconfigured or the file was copied from another project without regeneration. When `import content from "@/content/en.json"` fails with `Module not found`, fallback to relative imports: `import content from "../content/en.json"`. Apply this to ALL imports in ALL page and component files — header, footer, whatsapp-float, every page. The build error is a clean `Module not found: Can't resolve '@/content/en.json'` with file path. 2. **Next.js 15 `params` must be a Promise** ```tsx // ❌ BROKEN — Next.js < 15 export default function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { } // ✅ CORRECT — Next.js 15+ export default async function Page(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const params = await props.params } ``` Error: `Type '{ params: { slug: string; }; }' does not satisfy the constraint 'PageProps'. This is NOT a TypeScript config issue — it's a Next.js 15.3.x to 15.5.x bug that checks CSS imports even with `skipLibCheck: true`. The fix is to upgrade Next.js to latest (`npm install next@latest`) which resolves it in 15.5.18+. Without the upgrade, the only workaround is removing `import "./globals.css"` from layout.tsx — but this means Tailwind styles never load, making the site look like plain unstyled HTML. When using the template scaffold (no CSS import), the page must use inline style objects instead of Tailwind classes. This is acceptable for "Under Construction" placeholder pages but NOT for production. A production build always needs: ``` npm install next@latest tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss postcss ``` Plus a `postcss.config.mjs` with `{ plugins: { "@tailwindcss/postcss": {} } }`. **Gen 2 scaffold fix (the fast path):** When a freshly scaffolded app fails to build, the fastest path to a working 200 page is: 1. Remove `import "./globals.css"` from layout.tsx 2. Replace app/page.tsx with a self-contained inline-styled component 3. Remove the `@ai-whisperers/sections` import from package.json and app/page.tsx 4. Build + deploy as static page 5. Come back later to add real content + proper styling **Existing standalone clients:** | Client | Repo | URL | Method | |--------|------|-----|--------| | Dayah LitWorks | `Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks` | `dayah.paragu-ai.com` | Full build from dayah template | | El Viajero | `Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio` | `el-viajero.paragu-ai.com` | Full build from dayah template | | Nexa Paraguay | `Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay` | `nexa-paraguay.paragu-ai.com` | Custom with content resolver | | Fun4Me | `Ai-Whisperers/fun4me` | `fun4me.paragu-ai.com` | Template scaffold + inline page | | Alejandro Villamayor | `Ai-Whisperers/alejandro-villamayor` | `alejandro-villamayor.paragu-ai.com` | Template scaffold + inline page | **See also:** - `references/tenant-separation-model.md` — original nginx proxy approach (deprecated) - `references/standalone-client-deploy-pitfalls.md` — Docker Swarm SSL, stale containers, Next.js 15 traps - `references/standalone-nextjs-app-workflow.md` — building a full standalone app from scratch ## Platform Architecture Evolution Roadmap ### Current: Monolithic Monorepo (P0) `paragu-ai-builder/` — one repo, one Docker image, all tenants in `sites/`. # Dayah LitWorks — Agent Configuration

This repo contains the Dayah LitWorks website assets and deployment config.
The site runs as a standalone Docker service behind Traefik on the same VPS
as Paragu-AI Builder, at **dayah.paragu-ai.com**.

## Architecture

```
User → Cloudflare (DNS/CDN/SSL) → VPS → Traefik → dayah-litworks:3000 (nginx)
                                                          │
                                                          └→ proxy /s/es/dayah-litworks
                                                               to paragu-ai-builder_web:3000
```

The nginx container proxys requests to the main PAB backend which renders
the Dayah LitWorks site. This is a transitional architecture — eventually
the site will be a fully standalone Next.js app.

## How to Deploy

```bash
# 1. Build the image
docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .

# 2. Deploy as Docker Swarm service
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml dayah-litworks --detach=false
```

## Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `Dockerfile` | Builds nginx container |
| `nginx.conf` | Reverse proxy config (routes to PAB) |
| `docker-compose.yml` | Swarm stack with Traefik labels |
| `deploy.sh` | Full deploy script |
| `assets/data/` | Site content, tokens, page configs |
| `assets/images/` | Cover images, logos, SVGs |
| `docs/` | Business docs, style guides, improvement plans |

## Environment

- **Domain:** dayah.paragu-ai.com (staging), dayah-litworks.com (production)
- **VPS IP:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** v3.5.3 with LetsEncrypt (agent-net overlay network)
- **Proxy upstream:** paragu-ai-builder_web:3000 (Docker Swarm service)

## CRITICAL: Before Changing Content

The content lives in `assets/data/content-es.json` and `assets/data/content-en.json`.
These are NOT directly served — they must be copied into the PAB repo and rebuilt:

1. Update `assets/data/content-es.json` with new Spanish content
2. Copy to `paragu-ai-builder/web/lib/engine/dayah-litworks-data.ts`
3. Rebuild PAB: `docker build -f web/Dockerfile -t paragu-ai:prod` in PAB repo
4. Deploy PAB: `docker service update --force paragu-ai-builder_web`
5. If only nginx config changed, just rebuild this image and deploy:

   ```bash
   docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .
   docker service update --force dayah-litworks_web
   ```

## Future: Standalone Site

To make this repo fully standalone:

1. Add `paragu-ai-builder` as a git submodule under `engine/`
2. Create a minimal `app/layout.tsx` + `app/page.tsx` that imports the PAB
   section components and renders dayah's content
3. Change `Dockerfile` to build a Next.js standalone export instead of nginx
4. Remove the nginx reverse proxy dependency on PAB
This is the old nginx proxy approach (deprecated). The newer standalone approach is what Nexa/El Viajero use. Let me check the nexaparagua repo for the current standalone pattern.

# El Viajero — AI Agent Guide

Standalone Next.js 15 app at **https://viajero.paragu-ai.com**.
Outdoor/camping/fishing e-commerce store in Asuncion (est. 2018).

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://viajero.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio
- **Docker service:** elviajero-comercio_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** viajero.paragu-ai.com → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Architecture

User → Cloudflare (SSL) → VPS → Traefik → elviajero-comercio_web:3000

## Pages (12, all static)

| Route | Content | Built |
|-------|---------|-------|
| / | Hero + stats + features + categories + testimonials + newsletter + CTA + footer | ✅ |
| /tienda | Product catalog with categories (12 products, 6 cats) | ✅ |
| /productos | Category cards linking to /tienda#cat | ✅ |
| /nosotros | About story (3 paragraphs) + values (4) | ✅ |
| /contacto | Contact info + form → WhatsApp | ✅ |
| /faq | Accordion, 10 Q&A items | ✅ |
| /blog | 6 blog post cards + 4 categories | ✅ |
| /blog/[slug] | Individual post | ✅ |
| /promociones | 3 promotion cards with badges | ✅ |
| /privacidad | Privacy policy | ✅ |
| /terminos | Terms & conditions | ✅ |
| /sitemap.xml | SEO sitemap | ✅ |

## Design System

| Token | Value | Usage |
|-------|-------|-------|
| --color-primary | #1B5E20 | Buttons, headings, hero bg |
| --color-secondary | #37474F | Footer bg |
| --color-accent | #E65100 | Badges, promo bg |
| --color-background | #FAFAFA | Page bg |
| --color-surface | #FFFFFF | Cards |
| --color-foreground | #1A1A2E | Body text |
| --color-muted-foreground | #6B7280 | Secondary text |

## Content

**Editable file:** `content/es.json` — 505 lines, all text/pricing/products/FAQ.

Key sections:
- `home.hero` — headline + CTAs
- `home.productCatalog` — 12 products, 6 categories, WhatsApp order template
- `home.features` — 6 feature cards
- `home.testimonials` — 4 real client reviews
- `home.finalCta` — bottom CTA
- `faq` — title, hero, 10 items
- `promociones.promotions` — 3 promotions

## Build & Deploy

```bash
npm run build                          # Build Next.js
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false
```

**AFTER content changes** in content/es.json, the app imports it at build time. Run `npm run build` + rebuild Docker image.

## Critical Patterns

1. **No PAB paths** — all links use standalone routes (/, /tienda, /faq, etc.)
2. **Content-driven pages** — every page reads from content/es.json
3. **WhatsApp-first** — all product CTAs go to WhatsApp with prefilled messages using {{productName}} {{productPrice}} templates
4. **No external images** — all images are local SVGs or Unsplash URLs (can replace later)
5. **Light theme** — green + orange on white bg, outdoor/fresh feel

{"content": "FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 \
    CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://127.0.0.1:3000/ || exit 1
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
", "path": "/tmp/maiyu-atelier/Dockerfile"}

services:
  web:
    build: .

    ports:
      - "3000"
    networks:

    deploy:

      replicas: 2

        - "traefik.http.routers.maiyu.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.maiyu.rule=Host(`maiyu.paragu-ai.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.maiyu.tls=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.maiyu.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.maiyu.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s

networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true
", "path": "/tmp/maiyu-atelier/docker-compose.yml"}

Handcrafted crochet flowers & designs — roses, sunflowers, bells, strawberries, cats, bunnies.

**Live:** https://maiyu.paragu-ai.com

Then add A record in Cloudflare: `maiyu` → `72.61.44.159` (proxied).

Edit `content/es.json` for all text content. Then rebuild: `npm run build && docker build -t maiyu-atelier:prod . && docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml maiyu-atelier --detach=false`

Its content JSON (80KB/locale) uses a complex structure that the PAB engine normally resolves at runtime. For the standalone app, this was handled with two strategies: **Strategy 1: Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json` — 4KB, generated during build) The resolver (`lib/resolve.ts`) handles: 1. `{"$img": "programs.tierBase"}` → `/images/programs/tier-base.webp` (dot-path to file path) 2. Locale-aware objects `{"en": "...", "es": "..."}` → English value 3. Different data paths: `home.programs.tiers` (not `programs.items`), `home.whyCountry.pillars` (not `items`) 4. All nav/footer PAB paths batch-stripped **Strategy 2: Inline data for sub-pages** Pages like `/programs`, `/faq`, `/blog`, `/about` use hardcoded arrays instead of the content JSON, because the 80KB content structure has items nested at depths that don't match the component expectations. This is a pragmatic tradeoff — the JSON data is available for future content-management if the structure is normalized. **Key paths per page data source:** | Page | Data Source | Why | |------|-------------|-----| | Home | `content/resolved.json` | Resolver handles complex nesting + image refs | | /programs | Inline array (4 tiers) | Content has `programasPage.programs` as dict, not array | | /faq | Inline array (7 Q&A) | Content FAQ at `faqPage.full` with different structure | | /blog | Inline array (8 posts) | Blog posts in content are at `blog.index.posts` but empty | | /about | Inline HTML + array (6 cards) | About page has `differentiators` not `description` | **Reference:** `references/nexa-content-resolver-pattern.md` for the full resolver implementation, inline IMAGE_PATHS mapping, and locale resolution pipeline. ### Nexa Blog + Sub-Page Data Strategy The Nexa content JSON (80KB/locale) has blog posts, FAQ items, and program data nested at depths that DON'T match the page components. Two strategies emerged: **Strategy A — Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json`): For the HOME PAGE, pre-resolve all data at build time. The resolver handles `$img` refs, locale objects, and different data paths. Components import the resolved JSON (4KB) instead of the raw content (80KB). **Strategy B — Inline data for sub-pages** (programs, faq, blog, about): These pages use hardcoded arrays because the 80KB content structure has items in paths like `programasPage.programs` (dict, not array of items), FAQ items in `faqPage.full` (non-standard format), and blog posts in `blog.index.posts` (empty array in the JSON — actual posts are MDX files in a `blog/` directory). **When to use which:** ```typescript // Strategy A — content matches component expectations // Use resolved.json when you can flatten the data paths // Strategy B — content structure doesn't match // Use inline arrays when: // - Content JSON has empty arrays for expected fields // - Data is stored in objects instead of arrays // - Items use non-standard field names // - Content exists in external files (MDX, separate JSON) ``` **Blog post page with Next.js 15 async params (no MDX):** ```tsx // app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx const posts = [...]; // inline array export default async function BlogPost(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await props.params // MUST await params in Next.js 15 const post = posts.find(p => p.slug === slug) if (!post) notFound() // ... render post } ``` **Blog cards linking to [slug]:** ```tsx // app/blog/page.tsx — cards must be not

{p.title}

``` ### PNG Dedup for Docker Image Optimization After extracting images to a standalone app, run: ```bash find public/images -name "*.png" -delete ``` This saves 100-150MB. All PAB-generated images have both `.webp` (modern, used by components) and `.png` (older, unused fallback). The `.webp` versions are 10-20x smaller. Coming Soon

) } ``` This replaces 7+ individual `app/{page}/page.tsx` files with ONE dynamic route. Every undefined route returns 200 with a branded "Coming Soon" message instead of a 404. The catch-all route MUST be the LAST route file alphabetically — Next.js matches catch-all routes last. ### Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Running it at import time in components means every request loads and resolves 80KB of JSON. The resolved output is written to `content/resolved.json` and components import THAT file instead. The `lib/resolve.ts` file exists for regeneration, not runtime use. **Cleanup reference:** `references/standalone-asset-audit.md` for the full asset audit process (identifying PNG duplicates, orphan SVGs, unused brand files). ### Common Standalone App Pitfalls 1. **`delegate_task` subagent fails with 401** — OpenRouter/provider auth errors prevent parallel execution. When subagent calls fail, fall back to sequential execution directly in the main session. The subagent system uses a different model (claude-sonnet-4 by default) which may be misconfigured or hitting rate limits. If `delegate_task` returns `Error code: 401 - {'error': {'message': 'User not found.'}}`, debug with `hermes config get models` or work around with terminal + `python3 << 'PYEOF' ... PYEOF` for batch operations. 1a. **Navigation structure varies by tenant — NEVER assume array shape.** Different tenants store nav in completely different structures. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/s/en/nexa-paraguay" }] } ``` **Fix:** Check with `typeof content.navigation`. For object with `navItems`, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. For array, use `c.navigation || []`. 1b. **Nav hrefs may contain PAB paths (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas`).** After fixing the structure (step 1a), the href values themselves are wrong — they reference old PAB routes. Batch-replace in the header component: ```typescript const href = (n.href || n.path || "").replace("/s/en/nexa-paraguay", "") || "/" ``` Without this, clicking a nav link goes to `/sobre` (correct) but `prefetchNextRouter` on the `` prefetches the munged path (e.g. `/object Object`) causing `[object Object] 404` errors in console. The same `.replace()` must be applied in BOTH desktop and mobile nav loops. 1c. **PAB nav href prefixed with `/` + `.replace()` creates `//s/en/...` paths.** If your code does `"/" + href` BEFORE the replace, the link becomes `"//s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas"` which Next.js renders as `[object Object]`. The order must be: extract href → strip PAB prefix → prepend `/` if empty. 1d. **`@/` import alias may fail in freshly scaffolded apps.** The `@/*` path alias in tsconfig.json may not resolve correctly if `tsconfig.json` has `baseUrl` misconfigured or the file was copied from another project without regeneration. When `import content from "@/content/en.json"` fails with `Module not found`, fallback to relative imports: `import content from "../content/en.json"`. Apply this to ALL imports in ALL page and component files — header, footer, whatsapp-float, every page. The build error is a clean `Module not found: Can't resolve '@/content/en.json'` with file path. 2. **Next.js 15 `params` must be a Promise** ```tsx // ❌ BROKEN — Next.js < 15 export default function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { } // ✅ CORRECT — Next.js 15+ export default async function Page(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const params = await props.params } ``` Error: `Type '{ params: { slug: string; }; }' does not satisfy the constraint 'PageProps'. Types of property 'params' are incompatible.` - **Next.js 15 CSS import module resolution bug:** `Cannot find module or type declarations for side-effect import of './globals.css'` — This is a version-specific bug in Next.js 15.3.x with certain TypeScript configurations. Fix: **upgrade Next.js** (`npm install next@latest`) which installs 15.5+ and resolves the issue. The old fix (removing `import "./globals.css"` from layout.tsx) works but leaves Tailwind styles unloaded — the real fix is upgrading. Symptom: build succeeds but no CSS vars are applied in the browser. Also check for other Next-version-related issues like `next` binary not found in `npx next build` after copying node_modules from another project — do a clean `rm -rf node_modules && npm install`. 2. **Favicon must be in `app/` or referenced in metadata** Browser requests `/favicon.ico` by default. Put a file at `app/favicon.ico` or set `icons: { icon: "/favicon.svg" }` in `layout.tsx` metadata export. SVG files in `public/` won't be served as `.ico` unless explicitly referenced. 3. **Deploying a standalone app** ```bash cd /tmp/{client}/ npm run build docker build -t {client}:prod . docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {stack-name} ``` Then add A record in Cloudflare: `{client}` → `72.61.44.159` (proxied). Fix: add `'--foreground': colors.text` right after `--accent-foreground`. ## 🔴 CRITICAL: Hero Background Overlay Must Work on Dark Themes The hero component had baked `color-mix(in srgb, var(--primary) 70%, transparent)` as the overlay gradient. For dark sites with a LIGHT primary color (dayah: `--primary: #94abd6`), this overlay is translucent light-blue — **not enough contrast** for white-ish text. The fix was two-part: 1. **Component change** (`hero-section.tsx`): Replace the baked overlay with a configurable `overlayColor` prop, defaulting to `rgba(10,10,20,0.85)`: ```ts const backgroundStyle = backgroundImage && !useResponsivePicture ? { backgroundImage: `linear-gradient(${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}, ${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}), url(${backgroundImage})` } : undefined ``` 2. **Content change** (`es.json` / `en.json`): Add `"overlayColor": "rgba(10,10,20,0.88)"` to the hero content block. Without `overlayColor` in content, the component defaults to a dark overlay. This is NOT a TypeScript config issue — it's a Next.js 15.3.x to 15.5.x bug that checks CSS imports even with `skipLibCheck: true`.
